inline_review:
commit 945afd5cc3165b1526fa497432668ba20218f72b
Author: Chunyu Hu <chuhu@redhat.com>

selftests/mm: transhuge_stress: skip the test when thp not available

This commit skips the transhuge-stress test if transparent hugepages are not
available, to avoid false positive failures on systems with THP disabled.

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/transhuge-stress.c b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/transhuge-stress.c
> index bcad47c09518..7a9f1035099b 100644
> --- a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/transhuge-stress.c
> +++ b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/transhuge-stress.c

[ ... ]

> @@ -37,6 +38,9 @@ int main(int argc, char **argv)
>  
>  	ksft_print_header();
>  
> +	if (!thp_is_enabled())
> +		ksft_exit_skip("Transparent Hugepages not available\n");
> +

Could this early check inadvertently skip file-backed THP testing?

The test supports a -f <filename> argument to test file-backed memory
(like shmem/tmpfs). File-backed THP can be enabled independently of
anonymous THP (for example, via shmem_enabled).

Since this check happens before argument parsing, if a user attempts to run
the test against a file on a system where anonymous THP is disabled but
file-backed THP is enabled, the test will erroneously skip.

Would it be better to move this check after argument parsing, and perhaps
check the appropriate THP setting based on whether a file was provided?

>  	ram = sysconf(_SC_PHYS_PAGES);
>  	if (ram > SIZE_MAX / psize() / 4)
>  		ram = SIZE_MAX / 4;

commit 945afd5cc3165b1526fa497432668ba20218f72b
Author: Chunyu Hu <chuhu@redhat.com>
Date:   Tue Mar 24 01:33:16 2026 +0000

    selftests/mm: transhuge_stress: skip the test when thp not available
    
    The test requires thp, skip the test when thp is not available to avoid
    false positive.
    
    Tested with thp disabled kernel.
    Before the fix:
      # --------------------------------
      # running ./transhuge-stress -d 20
      # --------------------------------
      # TAP version 13
      # 1..1
      # transhuge-stress: allocate 1453 transhuge pages, using 2907 MiB virtual memory and 11 MiB of ram
      # Bail out! MADV_HUGEPAGE# Planned tests != run tests (1 != 0)
      # # Totals: pass:0 fail:0 xfail:0 xpass:0 skip:0 error:0
      # [FAIL]
      not ok 60 transhuge-stress -d 20 # exit=1
    
    After the fix:
      # --------------------------------
      # running ./transhuge-stress -d 20
      # --------------------------------
      # TAP version 13
      # 1..0 # SKIP Transparent Hugepages not available
      # [SKIP]
      ok 5 transhuge-stress -d 20 # SKIP
    
    Acked-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
    Reviewed-by: Mike Rapoport (Microsoft) <rppt@kernel.org>
    Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>
    Reviewed-by: Zi Yan <ziy@nvidia.com>
    CC: Li Wang <liwang@redhat.com>
    Signed-off-by: Chunyu Hu <chuhu@redhat.com>

diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/transhuge-stress.c b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/transhuge-stress.c
index bcad47c09518..7a9f1035099b 100644
--- a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/transhuge-stress.c
+++ b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/transhuge-stress.c
@@ -17,6 +17,7 @@
 #include <sys/mman.h>
 #include "vm_util.h"
 #include "kselftest.h"
+#include "thp_settings.h"
 
 int backing_fd = -1;
 int mmap_flags = MAP_ANONYMOUS | MAP_NORESERVE | MAP_PRIVATE;
@@ -37,6 +38,9 @@ int main(int argc, char **argv)
 
 	ksft_print_header();
 
+	if (!thp_is_enabled())
+		ksft_exit_skip("Transparent Hugepages not available\n");
+
 	ram = sysconf(_SC_PHYS_PAGES);
 	if (ram > SIZE_MAX / psize() / 4)
 		ram = SIZE_MAX / 4;

Okay, let's drill down into `thp_settings.c`:

```c
bool thp_is_enabled(void)
{
	if (!thp_available())
		return false;

	int mode = thp_read_string("enabled", thp_enabled_strings);

	/* THP is considered enabled if it's either "always" or "madvise" */
	return mode == 1 || mode == 3;
}
```

This is where the magic happens.  If `thp_available()` returns true (directory exists) and the THP mode is "madvise" (mode 3) or "always" (mode 1), `thp_is_enabled()` returns `true`. If THP is set to "never", which would make `mode` some other value, `thp_is_enabled()` returns false.

Here's the critical piece of insight: If THP is globally set to "never" via `/sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled`, `madvise(MADV_HUGEPAGE)` will *not* override this. `madvise(MADV_HUGEPAGE)` *only* works if `enabled` is either `madvise` or `always`. If it's "never," `madvise` won't allocate THP pages, and the test would fail. Therefore, skipping the test when THP is globally disabled is the correct thing to do because `madvise` would fail to allocate the pages.

That's clean.

The primary concern raised by the patch is the addition of the `thp_is_enabled()` check.  It skips the test if Transparent Hugepages (THP) aren't available. The commit message rightly points out the need to avoid false positives. I'm going to investigate this thoroughly.

My initial thought was that even if THP is "never" enabled system-wide, we might still allocate huge pages using `MADV_HUGEPAGE`. However, the documentation for `/sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled` clearly states that "never" means `MADV_HUGEPAGE` will *not* enable THP. So, the check in `thp_is_enabled()` correctly skips the test if THP is explicitly disabled.

Essentially, `thp_is_enabled()` looks at `/sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled`. If it's "never", the test needs to be skipped. And that's exactly what the code does, checking for `mode == 1 || mode == 3` (always or madvise).

I need to confirm no resources are leaked. The `ksft_exit_skip()` function calls `exit()`, and that the skip happens *before* `mmap()` call. This ensures there are no leaks.

I'm digging deep, looking for any hidden issues. Let's see if there are any side effects of the `thp_is_enabled()` function itself. It calls `thp_read_string()`, which simply reads the `/sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled` file.  It's pure and safe. The check is completely correct, in-line with standard kselftest practices.

Now, what about command-line arguments? If `-h` (help) is passed and THP is disabled, the program exits with SKIP before printing usage. This is slightly unusual CLI behavior, but irrelevant for automated selftests because the runner won't pass `-h`.

Is `sysconf(_SC_PHYS_PAGES)` valid? Yes, it's POSIX. This seems good.

In conclusion, I can't find anything fundamentally wrong. The patch is sound and necessary to avoid false positives in environments where THP is intentionally disabled. It appears to be a well-considered and correct addition to the kselftests.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the THP Stress Test and KSFT Skip Logic**

Okay, let's break this down. It seems the issue is that the `transhuge-stress` test is failing because it's bailing out before it even attempts to allocate THPs. My initial thought was that a failure in `allocate_transhuge` was the root cause, returning -1. But then, it became clear that the test's failure is tied to the `madvise(MADV_HUGEPAGE)` call. When `madvise` fails, it exits with the "MADV_HUGEPAGE" error. The commit message confirms this: The test immediately fails with MADV_HUGEPAGE, as the plan's tests don't match the tests run.

The core of the problem lies in `transhuge-stress.c`'s `madvise` call, which is immediately failing. The quick solution to this is to check if THP is even enabled. This test does account for this with the `thp_is_enabled()` check. So, the question is how the KSFT framework handles this.

Now, the use of `ksft_print_header()` followed by `ksft_exit_skip()` is the right pattern here. That ensures a clean exit if THP is unavailable. The code prints "TAP version 13" followed by the appropriate "1..0 # SKIP" message. The test runner recognizes this TAP output as a skip. Tracing `ksft_exit_skip` confirms this: It prints the SKIP message and exits. Then `ksft_print_cnts()` gets called, which prints the test results, including the `skip:0` count. 

I was initially concerned about whether `ksft_exit_skip` was correctly incrementing the skip counter, but the output `1..0 # SKIP` is sufficient for the test harness to interpret it as a skip.

I also checked if the use of `sysconf(_SC_PHYS_PAGES)` after the `thp_is_enabled()` check was a potential issue. It is not.

Finally, I reviewed the dependencies, particularly the inclusion of `thp_settings.h` and its reliance on `stdbool.h`. It is all handled correctly. `thp_settings.h` takes care of including `stdbool.h` which is good practice. This is a properly implemented test using the KSFT framework. The core failure is not an unexpected condition - it is working as designed.

Now, let's nail down this `thp_is_enabled()` function. It seems pretty straightforward: if THP isn't "always" or "madvise", it returns false. If it's "inherit", *that* seems odd. Hold on a second, is "inherit" even a valid option for `enabled`? Let's check `thp_enabled_strings`:

```c
static const char * const thp_enabled_strings[] = {
	"never",
	"always",
	"inherit",
	"madvise",
	NULL
};
```

Ah, there it is! "inherit" is indeed in the mix, at index 2. This means that if `enabled` is set to "inherit", then `mode` will equal 2. And, yes, the function `thp_is_enabled()` *then* returns `mode == 1 || mode == 3;`, so "always" or "madvise". *But what does "inherit" mean in the context of the THP enabled sysfs*?

Thinking about `/sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled`, the standard options are "always [madvise] never" or "[always] madvise never". *That* top-level `enabled` doesn't have "inherit". Hold on, I see `thp_enabled_strings` is used for both the top-level `/sys` file and the per-size `enabled`. Ah, if you read the top-level file, you'll see something like "always [madvise] never". It parses the active mode - the one in brackets. Since "inherit" isn't in the file, `strchr(buf, '[')` will find the brackets. Then, if `[always]` is present, `mode` is 1; if `[madvise]`, mode is 3; if `[never]`, mode is 0. So, `mode == 1 || mode == 3` correctly translates to "always" or "madvise". It all makes sense! It seems the logic in `thp_is_enabled()` is perfectly sound.

Now, let's see if the patch is missing any other pieces. Here, it is:


Now, about `ksft_print_header` and `ksft_exit_skip`? I know we normally `ksft_set_plan(X)` before starting tests or exiting, but `ksft_exit_skip` allows `ksft_plan == 0` and dynamically prints `1..0 # SKIP ` in TAP 14 standard way to skip all tests. Wait, TAP 13/14 format says `1..0 # SKIP message` skips all tests. And the patch shows:
```
# TAP version 13
1..0 # SKIP Transparent Hugepages not available
```
This is *correct*.

Okay, let me break this down. The commit message says "Tested with thp disabled kernel," which means `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE` is off. In that case, `madvise` will return `-EINVAL`, as I recall from earlier. This is the crux of the issue.

Right, so if THP is *disabled* in the kernel build, the `/sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage` directory *won't exist*. Therefore, `thp_available` returns `false`, `thp_is_enabled()` also returns `false`, and the test properly *skips*. Perfect; it handles the "thp disabled" scenario spot-on.

Now, what if THP *is* compiled into the kernel but the user booted with `transparent_hugepage=never` (disabling it via sysfs)? In this case, `thp_is_enabled()` *still* returns `false`, and the test *still* skips. Is that correct behavior? Well, yeah, with "never," you're explicitly preventing transparent huge page allocation. The stress test would just fail at every attempt to allocate transhuge pages ( `allocate_transhuge` returning `-1` for every page). The test loop would just increment `nr_failed++` for all pages. It wouldn't crash, but it also wouldn't meaningfully test anything because everything would fail. So, skipping the test makes total sense here.

Hold on a second... Could `thp_is_enabled()` incorrectly skip the test when THP is enabled per-VMA (but not system-wide)? Recall `transparent_hugepage/enabled = madvise`. That means it's enabled on a per-VMA basis when `MADV_HUGEPAGE` is used. But `thp_is_enabled` specifically checks if the mode is `1` ("always") OR `3` ("madvise"). So, if it's "madvise," `thp_is_enabled()` returns `true`, and the test *runs*! That's exactly the intended behavior. Great, it handles that case correctly.

Wait, is there any case where `thp_is_enabled()` might exit the *entire* program if it can't read the sysfs file? Yes, `thp_read_string()` calls `exit(EXIT_FAILURE)` if reading the file fails. Is that okay? If the directory exists, but the "enabled" file is missing, it exits with an error. This implies the kernel is in an unexpected state (maybe a newer kernel where the file is renamed or gone). As this is a selftest, dying with an error message makes sense if sysfs is corrupted/changed.

Finally, what if `thp_available()` is called, and `access()` returns `-1` because we don't have permissions, or the sysfs is not mounted? If sysfs isn't mounted, well, there's not much to do. What if `transhuge-stress` is run by a *normal user*? Can a regular user read `/sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled`? I need to check the permissions of `enabled` to be certain.

Okay, let's break this down. My initial thought process is centered around ensuring the proposed patch behaves correctly and doesn't introduce any regressions or unexpected side effects.

First, I confirmed the test's ability to determine if THP is enabled. The `enabled` attribute's permissions (readable by all, writable by root) and the system directory's permissions mean a standard user *can* read the status. The `thp_available` check, using `access()` on `/sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage`, also works as expected, confirming the test can reliably detect THP availability.

Then, I focused on the `kselftests` skip mechanism, specifically `ksft_exit_skip`. My concern was the test skipping condition, as I was curious if anything in the original code would affect that skip behavior. I confirmed that `ksft_plan` not being set to anything (remaining as 0 initially) will cause the appropriate output ( `1..0 # SKIP ...` ). The fix places this skip before argument parsing, which is the correct placement. The original code does not allocate memory before skipping so it's not a concern. The fix also correctly formats the skip message with the newline character, so it displays correctly.

My next line of thought then went to different edge cases: what if THP *is* available but other arguments like `duration` or the `name` (file) are passed? In this case, the test should continue running the same way as before. This is indeed the case - there's no behavioral change for systems with THP enabled.

I then revisited the `thp_is_enabled()` function which calls `thp_read_string()`, which opens `/sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled`. The core of my analysis boils down to assessing if there are any missing API contracts or semantic errors. After careful review, I'm confident there are none. The commit accomplishes its stated purpose, which is to skip the test when THP is unavailable. It utilizes the standardized `kselftest` THP helper, `thp_is_enabled()`, which is consistent with other memory management self-tests. The commit message's example output clearly illustrates the change. Multiple reviewers have vetted the logic, and it seems sound.

Finally, I wanted to double-check the build process and make sure `thp_settings.o` is correctly linked, as it seemed as though only dependencies were mentioned. The Makefile in the tools/testing/selftests/mm directory confirms the build is done correctly.

In summary, my deep dive has yielded a high level of confidence that this patch is correct, safe, and implements the intended functionality without introducing any issues. I'm satisfied.

Okay, so I'm seeing `ksft_exit_skip()` being called *way* before `ksft_set_plan()` in the codebase. That's a bit odd, usually I'd expect a plan to be established first. Let's dig into the implementation, specifically the header file `kselftest.h`. Hmm, here it is:

```c
static inline __noreturn __printf(1, 2) void ksft_exit_skip(const char *msg, ...)
{
	int saved_errno = errno;
	va_list args;

	va_start(args, msg);

	/*
	 * FIXME: several tests misuse ksft_exit_skip so produce
	 * something sensible if some tests have already been run
	 * or a plan has been printed.  Those tests should use
	 * ksft_test_result_skip or ksft_exit_fail_msg instead.
	 */
	if (ksft_plan || ksft_test_num()) {
		ksft_cnt.ksft_xskip++;
		printf("ok %u # SKIP ", 1 + ksft_test_num());
	} else {
		printf("1..0 # SKIP ");
	}
```

Alright, I see it. It outputs `1..0 # SKIP ` if no plan has been set or tests run, which is totally TAP compliant, that's smart! This avoids issues with undefined plan and test numbers. Then, it'll output the supplied message, which makes sense. I see it uses `printf`, but the `vprintf` isn't shown above... guess it's included in the function's internals.

Specifically, I'm reviewing this `transhuge-stress.c` file. The primary concern seems to be this check:

```c
	if (!thp_is_enabled())
		ksft_exit_skip("Transparent Hugepages not available\n");
```

My initial thought was, could `thp_is_enabled` falsely return `false` if there's a permission issue reading the sysfs file? But no, this test generally runs as root, so file permissions for reading shouldn't be a problem.

Wait, looking closer at the context, this test *definitely* needs THP. It's explicitly calling `madvise(..., MADV_HUGEPAGE)` and then allocating transhuge pages. There's no way around it.

Alright, so the real concern is ensuring there are no resource leaks, but since `ksft_exit_skip` just exits the whole process, the OS cleans everything up. Also, the check is at the very beginning of `main`, so there won't be much allocated anyway.

Aha! Now I see something I didn't catch at first. Let's delve into `thp_is_enabled` in `thp_settings.c`:

```c
bool thp_is_enabled(void)
{
	if (!thp_available())

	int mode = thp_read_string("enabled", thp_enabled_strings);

	/* THP is considered enabled if it's either "always" or "madvise" */
	return mode == 1 || mode == 3;
}
```

    "thought": "**Analysis of Proposed THP Test Enhancement**

Okay, here's the breakdown of my thinking on this patch, as an expert in this area:

Initially, my concern was the potential for `thp_read_string` to cause an abrupt exit (due to `exit(EXIT_FAILURE)`) if it couldn't read the "enabled" setting. My quick thought was that it could lead to premature test failure instead of the intended skip.

However, I quickly realized that `thp_available` only checks the existence of the `/sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/` directory. Then, if that exists, the test subsequently calls `thp_is_enabled` which then calls `thp_read_string` which reads `/sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled`. If this read fails, `thp_read_string` does indeed exit.  The major point is that it is the *same behavior* as existed previously for `MADV_HUGEPAGE`, which would also have failed the test if THP was disabled (or otherwise unavailable).

I then considered different kernel configurations: What if THP is completely unsupported (no sysfs directory), or partially disabled. If the THP sysfs directory doesn't exist, `thp_available()` returns false, and the test skips, which is the correct behavior.  If THP is compiled but disabled (e.g., `never` mode), `thp_read_string` will return false, the test will skip.  Before this patch, it would continue and fail at `MADV_HUGEPAGE`, which is worse.

I checked for any memory allocation or locking issues within the `thp_settings.c` code, but found none.

I went back to the `transhuge-stress.c` code and realized a potential "minor annoyance" with the `-h` (help) option. If THP is unavailable, the test now skips *before* printing the help message. I concluded this is an acceptable tradeoff, as properly skipping is more important than printing help in a test environment.

Dependency-wise, I confirmed that `thp_settings.c` is compiled and linked. Then I checked that the test targets Transparent Hugepages, and not the other variety of hugepages, so that's all good.

I double-checked `thp_available()` and confirmed it checks `F_OK` on `/sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/`, which is as expected. Sysfs is almost always mounted in selftests.

I carefully reviewed the introduced code:

```c

		ksft_exit_skip("Transparent Hugepages not available\n");
```

Next, I did a thorough check that no other part of the `transhuge-stress.c` code was using THP, so this check is all that was added.

My next step was to verify that it is safe to call `ksft_exit_skip` before `ksft_set_plan`. I saw several examples in the source, so that's no issue.

If `ksft_set_plan` is not called, the output will include `1..0 # SKIP`, which is exactly what the patch and TAP expects.

I next considered the scenario of `thp_is_enabled` giving the wrong answer, but I quickly confirmed the checks are correct with the current valid THP values.

I then went over the function call stack to be absolutely sure, confirming the new `thp_is_enabled` and its dependents.

I then did a deep-dive into potential `thp_read_string` failure cases. I checked that it reads `/sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled`, and if that fails the test exits, which is fine and expected behavior.     "thought": "**Summary of `transhuge-stress.c` Patch Analysis**

Okay, so I've been digging into this patch for `transhuge-stress.c`, and here's my take: the goal is to skip the test when THP isn't available, which is sensible to prevent those nasty `MADV_HUGEPAGE` failures. The script `run_vmtests.sh` runs the test by default without any `-f` options, therefore, the test targets anonymous THP.  The core of the patch adds a check using `thp_is_enabled()` early in `main()`. This seems fine at first glance.  If THP isn't enabled globally (based on the `/sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled` setting, which is used by `thp_is_enabled()`), the test skips.  That should be the intention, because the `madvise(MADV_HUGEPAGE)` call will fail and crash when THP is completely unavailable (built without `CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE`), or the test will just return 0 succeed if THP is never, therefore, the check prevents the test from running unnecessarily.

However, here's where it gets interesting and where I see a potential issue: This check is done *before* the arguments are parsed. The test *does* support a `-f` option, allowing users to specify a file (like a tmpfs mount) for file-backed THP testing.  File-backed THP relies on the `shmem_enabled` setting or other filesystem settings for large folios, not the global `enabled` setting.  Thus, if a user specifies `-f /dev/shm/some_file`, but anonymous THP is set to `never`, the test will be skipped *incorrectly*. While anonymous THP can be never, and file-backed can be always, which makes the check too restrictive and will break file-backed THP tests, as the logic will exit. This is definitely a regression and breaks the intended usage of the test. Ideally, the check needs to be placed *after* the command-line arguments are parsed and use the proper THP check (`shmem_enabled`) based on whether `-f` was provided. It is not possible to determine if this is a critical regression, but should definitely be mentioned.

There's another potential pitfall with the `-h` option. If the system has THP disabled, running `./transhuge-stress -h` will *also* skip the test, and the user won't see the usage information, which is a common `-h` command feature.  However, this behavior is a side-effect, and doesn't appear to be a serious issue because it just fails with a TAP format. I'm not going to flag this as a major concern because it is valid, despite being less than ideal.

The patch's argument parsing remains unchanged, and it doesn't appear to introduce any memory leaks or other significant issues. I verified the skip mechanism using TAP and confirms that skipping at the start is expected with the test format.  The test correctly prevents `MADV_HUGEPAGE` from failing, therefore, there is no issue regarding those cases.

In short, while the patch correctly addresses the "THP not available" scenario for anonymous THP tests, it introduces a logic regression regarding the file-backed tests by skipping them unnecessarily.

selftests/mm: transhuge_stress: skip the test when thp not available

This commit skips the transhuge-stress test if transparent hugepages are not
available, to avoid false positive failures on systems with THP disabled.

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/mm/transhuge-stress.c b/tools/testing/selftests/mm/transhuge-stress.c

>  
> +	if (!thp_is_enabled())
> +		ksft_exit_skip("Transparent Hugepages not available\n");
> +

Could this early check inadvertently skip file-backed THP testing?

The test supports a -f <filename> argument to test file-backed memory
(like shmem/tmpfs). File-backed THP can be enabled independently of
anonymous THP (for example, via shmem_enabled).

Since this check happens before argument parsing, if a user attempts to run
the test against a file on a system where anonymous THP is disabled but
file-backed THP is enabled, the test will erroneously skip.

Would it be better to move this check after argument parsing, and perhaps
check the appropriate THP setting based on whether a file was provided?

>  	ram = sysconf(_SC_PHYS_PAGES);
>  	if (ram > SIZE_MAX / psize() / 4)
